metadata.title: Build an SCCT Crisis Triage Tool with Claude
metadata.note: cli-ideas Card 16 — Ch 16 (Crisis and Reputation Management). DOUBLE-CHECK: SCCT = Coombs 2007 'Protecting Organization Reputations During a Crisis'. Three types: Victim → Deny/Bolster, Accidental → Diminish, Preventable → Rebuild/Mortification. Tylenol: pulled 31M bottles, $100M cost, 35% → 8% → recovered above pre-crisis by 1984. United Airlines: April 9 2017 video, CEO statement next morning. BP: 11 workers killed, 4.9M barrels, 87 days, Hayward resigned July 2010. STRATEGY MISMATCH WARNING fires when lower-responsibility strategy applied to higher-responsibility crisis. Every statement is DRAFT — human approval required.

beats.role_note: COLD OPEN LAW — Claude UI, ask shown answered; IN-FOR-BEAR
beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Build it with Claude, then take it apart.

props.command: claude "classify this crisis, select SCCT strategy, draft holding statement"
output: Crisis type: Preventable — high public responsibility
STRATEGY MISMATCH WARNING: diminish strategy on a preventable crisis compounds damage
Holding statement: DRAFT — human approval required

beats.role_note: stakes — BEFORE CLI loop; SHOW-DON'T-TELL: animate the Tylenol vs BP comparison
beats.narration_text: United Airlines lost the first day of the narrative without saying anything at all. The video of a passenger being dragged off the plane was posted on April ninth, 2017. It had been seen by millions before the end of the day. United's statement came the following morning — after the framing had already been established. The CEO defended the airline's right to remove disruptive passengers. That was the wrong strategy on a high-responsibility crisis. W. Timothy Coombs codified this in his Situational Crisis Communication Theory: the right response depends on how much responsibility the public assigns to the organization. Apply the wrong strategy to the wrong crisis type and the response compounds the damage. This tool classifies the crisis, selects the strategy, and fires a mismatch warning before anything goes out.
beats.visual_intent: Timeline: April 9 2017 — video posted, 1M views, CEO statement (next morning) — gap labeled 'narrative captured without you'; then two-column: Tylenol 1982 (Victim, Rebuild → 35%→8%→recovery) vs BP Deepwater 2010 (Preventable, Diminish → Hayward resigned July 2010); no composer yet

beats.role_note: ClaudeComposerAsk — show and discuss the prompt
beats.narration_text: Into Claude. Given this crisis description, step one: classify as Victim, Accidental, or Preventable, with a rationale citing specific facts. Step two: select the correct response strategy — Victim maps to Deny or Bolster, Accidental to Diminish, Preventable to Rebuild and Mortification. If the description contains language suggesting a lower-responsibility strategy on a higher-responsibility crisis, fire the STRATEGY MISMATCH WARNING. Step three: draft a holding statement calibrated to the crisis type. Preventable crises accept responsibility without equivocation. Step four: name the escalation path — who decides, who speaks, whether legal review is required. Every statement is DRAFT, human approval required.

props.command:
claude "triage this crisis:
  [CRISIS DESCRIPTION]
  Step 1: SCCT type — Victim / Accidental / Preventable
    + rationale citing specific facts
  Step 2: strategy — Victim→Deny/Bolster,
    Accidental→Diminish, Preventable→Rebuild/Mortification
    If mismatch detected: STRATEGY MISMATCH WARNING
  Step 3: holding statement — DRAFT — human approval req.
    (Preventable: accept responsibility without equivocation)
  Step 4: escalation — who decides, who speaks,
    legal review required?"

beats.role_note: ClaudeCodeBeat — actual triage script; trimmed to the lines that teach
beats.narration_text: Read it. The mismatch detection logic is the line that earns its place: if the description contains language suggesting a diminish strategy — context, extenuating factors, cost constraints — and the classification comes back Preventable, the warning fires. That pattern is exactly what BP's response did: applied diminish to a preventable crisis. The holding statement function selects the template by crisis type. Preventable template leads with acceptance. That is the only viable path when public responsibility attribution is high and rising.

props.title: crisis_triage.py
props.code:
# crisis_triage.py  —  Claude Code output
import anthropic, sys

SYSTEM = """You are an SCCT crisis classifier (Coombs 2007).
Step 1: classify — Victim / Accidental / Preventable.
Step 2: strategy:
  Victim    → Deny or Bolster
  Accidental → Diminish
  Preventable → Rebuild / Mortification
If description uses diminish language on a Preventable
crisis, print:
  'STRATEGY MISMATCH WARNING: [lower strategy] on
   [higher type] crisis compounds damage.'
Step 3: holding statement — type-calibrated, DRAFT.
  Preventable: accept responsibility, no equivocation.
Step 4: escalation — decision-maker, spokesperson,
  legal review (always yes for Preventable).
All statements: DRAFT — human approval required."""

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
result = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-7", max_tokens=2048,
    system=SYSTEM,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": sys.stdin.read()}]
)
print(result.content[0].text)
props.sparkLine: Strategy must match the crisis type.

beats.role_note: screen-rec or animated slot — Preventable crisis fires WARNING
beats.narration_text: Run it on a crisis description matching BP's Deepwater Horizon pattern — safety concerns overridden for cost and schedule. Preventable. Strategy: Rebuild, Mortification. Then run it again with a suggested diminish framing — the description notes cost constraints as context. The MISMATCH WARNING fires: applying Diminish to a Preventable crisis compounds damage. The holding statement prints with responsibility accepted in the first sentence, no equivocation. Legal review marked required.
beats.visual_intent: screen-rec mp4: crisis description typed in; classification prints 'Preventable — HIGH public responsibility'; then in red: 'STRATEGY MISMATCH WARNING: Diminish on Preventable crisis compounds damage'; holding statement prints with 'DRAFT — human approval required'; escalation path shows 'Legal review: REQUIRED'.

beats.narration_text: Now test the Victim path. Run the 1982 Tylenol crisis: product tampered with after leaving J-and-J's factories, an unknown criminal, the company did nothing wrong. The classification should come back Victim. Strategy: Bolster. The holding statement should lead with consumer safety — what J-and-J actually did — not with the company's innocence. Run both and compare the holding-statement register side by side.

props.command:
claude "triage the 1982 Tylenol crisis:
  Tampered product — poisoned after leaving J&J factories
  Unknown criminal, company had no prior knowledge
  Verify: Victim classification + Bolster strategy
  Holding statement should lead with consumer safety
  NOT with company's legal innocence.
  Compare output register to the Preventable
  template from prior run."

beats.role_note: screen-rec or animated slot — Victim classification vs Preventable side-by-side
beats.narration_text: Tylenol prints: Victim — low public responsibility. Strategy: Bolster. Holding statement leads with consumer safety, references the recall action before any mention of the company's position. Compare to the Preventable template: both accept the gravity of the situation, but Victim does not lead with apology. That register difference is the SCCT mechanism in practice. The strategy must match the crisis type — and J-and-J's response worked precisely because they did not lead with 'this is not our fault,' even though it was not.
beats.visual_intent: Two-panel comparison: left — Preventable crisis (red background) → Rebuild/Mortification → holding statement accepting responsibility; right — Victim crisis (amber) → Bolster → holding statement leading with consumer safety action. SCCT matrix visible between panels.

beats.narration_text: The strategic error that ruins most corporate crisis responses is applying the wrong strategy to the crisis type. It happens because high-responsibility crises are the most threatening — the largest liability, the most pressure from legal counsel to minimize disclosed responsibility. The instinct to treat a Preventable crisis as Accidental is exactly backward. Audiences read organizations by what they do under pressure. The mismatch warning is the mechanism that catches that instinct before it ships.
beats.visual_intent: Summary card: SCCT three-row matrix — Victim, Accidental, Preventable — with strategies; STRATEGY MISMATCH WARNING highlighted; Tylenol and BP as canonical contrast; bottom: 'correct triage at the first hour is worth a decade of goodwill'

beats.role_note: HANDOFF LAW — prompt READ aloud AND discussed; greeting 'Your turn.'
beats.narration_text: Your turn. Before any crisis arrives, build your playbook: describe the three most realistic crisis scenarios for your brand, paste each into the triage tool, and save the holding statement output for each type. The DRAFT label is non-negotiable — the tool produces holding statements for you to edit, approve, and sign off on. For Preventable scenarios, build the legal review step into your escalation path in advance. The holding statement you write today is the one you will be grateful for when someone is waiting for a response and the clock is at hour one.

props.command:
claude "triage three realistic crisis scenarios for my brand:
  Brand: [DESCRIPTION]
  Scenario 1: [MOST LIKELY CRISIS]
  Scenario 2: [MODERATE RISK CRISIS]
  Scenario 3: [HIGH-RESPONSIBILITY SCENARIO]
  For each: SCCT type, strategy, holding statement DRAFT,
  escalation path. Flag mismatch if present."

beats.narration_text: Build an SCCT Crisis Triage Tool with Claude. This is Liam, in for Bear — build it with Claude, then take it apart.
